Parameters:

```json
{
  "name": "halifax-now-ux-content-roadmap",
  "overview": "Design a UX and content roadmap for Halifax Now that builds on the existing GSD technical work and Claude roadmaps, with a strong focus on UI/UX and content for the upcoming summer season.",
  "plan": "## Halifax Now – UX \u0026 Content Roadmap\n\n### Goals\n\n- Create a clear, opinionated plan for Halifax Now’s **UI/UX** and **content experience** on top of the working scraper pipeline and calendar.\n- Align with your existing GSD plans and Claude roadmaps, while focusing especially on: \n  - Improving the **front-end experience** (cards, filters, navigation, mobile).\n  - Designing a **content strategy** (guides, themes, seasonal campaigns) ahead of summer.\n\n---\n\n### 1. Consolidate Existing Roadmaps \u0026 Constraints\n\n- **1.1 Collect planning artifacts**\n  - Review GSD artifacts in &#96;.planning/&#96; (PROJECT, ROADMAP, phase CONTEXT/PLAN/SUMMARY files) to understand what’s already promised for v1/v2.\n  - Review any separate Claude roadmap / notes you have (even if they’re informal) and identify overlapping ideas.\n\n- **1.2 Extract a single source of truth for direction**\n  - From those docs, pull out:\n    - Confirmed phases/features already implemented (pipeline, TEC setup, calendar pages).\n    - Planned but not yet started UX/content work (e.g. guides, neighbourhood pages, happy hour, SEO).\n  - Summarize into a short “Direction Brief” with:\n    - Who Halifax Now is for.\n    - What the site promises users.\n    - Which big bets matter for the next 6–12 months.\n\n---\n\n### 2. Define UX Principles \u0026 IA (Information Architecture)\n\n- **2.1 UX principles tailored to Halifax Now**\n  - Define 4–6 principles that will guide all UI decisions, for example:\n    - \"Tonight first\": always make it trivial to find what’s on tonight.\n    - \"Glanceable\": cards and lists should be scannable in 2–3 seconds.\n    - \"Neighbourhood‑aware\": make it easy to browse by area.\n    - \"No dead ends\": every event page suggests related events.\n\n- **2.2 Core navigation and IA structure**\n  - Lock in the main entry points in the nav:\n    - &#96;Tonight&#96;, &#96;This Weekend&#96;, &#96;All Events&#96;, &#96;Categories&#96;, &#96;Neighbourhoods&#96; (and later: &#96;Guides&#96;, &#96;Happy Hour&#96;).\n  - Design a simple sitemap showing:\n    - Calendar views (list, month, photo).\n    - Category hubs (e.g. &#96;Live Music&#96;, &#96;Comedy&#96;, &#96;Family \u0026 Kids&#96;).\n    - Neighbourhood hubs (e.g. &#96;Downtown&#96;, &#96;North End&#96;, &#96;Dartmouth&#96;).\n    - Future guide content (e.g. &#96;Summer Festivals&#96;, &#96;Patios&#96;, &#96;Rainy Day Ideas&#96;).\n\n- **2.3 Validate with your roadmap**\n  - Check this IA against your existing GSD roadmap and Claude roadmap so we don’t conflict with other planned phases (e.g. neighbourhood guides, happy hour section).\n\n---\n\n### 3. Event Card \u0026 Detail Page UX (Summer‑Ready)\n\n- **3.1 Standardize card layout**\n  - Specify exactly what each card shows and in what order:\n    - Category label, title, date/time, venue + neighbourhood, quick price indicator, 1–2 line description.\n  - Define two card variants:\n    - **Compact list card** for &#96;/events&#96;, &#96;/tonight&#96;, &#96;/this-weekend&#96;.\n    - **Featured card** for guides/homepage spots (larger image, more emphasis).\n\n- **3.2 Design event detail layout**\n  - Above the fold: title, date/time, venue (link), category, price, primary CTA (\"Get Tickets\" or source link).\n  - Below the fold: description, photos, map, tags, related events (by venue / category / date).\n  - Decide where to show **source attribution** (e.g. \"via Ticketmaster\" or venue link) in a subtle but clear way.\n\n- **3.3 Mobile-first checks**\n  - Ensure card and detail layouts work for small screens:\n    - Single-column cards, comfortable tap targets.\n    - Filters accessible via a simple toggle or sticky bar.\n\n---\n\n### 4. Filters, Search, and Date-Specific Views\n\n- **4.1 Filter design**\n  - Confirm the minimum filter set for &#96;/events&#96;, &#96;/tonight&#96;, &#96;/this-weekend&#96;:\n    - Category, Date Range, Neighbourhood, Venue, and optionally Price band (Free, \u003c$20, $20–$50, 50+).\n  - Document behaviour for combining filters and resetting them.\n\n- **4.2 “Tonight” and “This Weekend” UX**\n  - Lock in business rules:\n    - Tonight = all events today (already implemented via shortcode) but decide whether to emphasize evening events visually.\n    - This Weekend = Fri–Sun (already handled by &#96;[hfxnow_this_weekend]&#96;) — document this so content and marketing are aligned.\n  - Design pleasant **empty states** for quiet nights (show a message plus a few upcoming highlights instead of a blank list).\n\n- **4.3 Search improvements**\n  - For now, rely on TEC’s built-in search plus filters; in a later phase, consider a custom search experience that:\n    - Searches by title, venue, neighbourhood, and tags.\n    - Surfaces popular queries (\"brunch\", \"drag\", \"kids\").\n\n---\n\n### 5. Visual Design System (Lightweight but Reusable)\n\n- **5.1 Design tokens**\n  - Define a small set of design tokens you can apply via CSS:\n    - Color palette (brand, accent, neutrals).\n    - Typography scale (H1–H4, body, labels).\n    - Spacing units (e.g. 4/8/16/24 px).\n  - Document these in a simple &#96;DESIGN.md&#96; or similar for future reference.\n\n- **5.2 Core components**\n  - Specify styles for:\n    - Buttons (primary, secondary, text).\n    - Category chips / filters.\n    - Event cards, badges (\"Free\", \"Sold Out\").\n  - Keep this minimal but consistent so any new CSS you add later slots into the system instead of being ad‑hoc.\n\n---\n\n### 6. Content Strategy for Summer\n\n- **6.1 Define priority themes for summer**\n  - Decide the 3–5 most important seasonal content clusters, for example:\n    - Summer festivals \u0026 outdoor concerts.\n    - Patios, rooftop bars, waterfront events.\n    - Family \u0026 kids summer activities.\n    - Free and low-cost events.\n    - Neighbourhood‑specific summer guides.\n\n- **6.2 Plan guide pages and landing pages**\n  - For each theme, design:\n    - A simple landing page (guide) that mixes:\n      - Curated events from your pipeline.\n      - Short editorial intros, tips, and links.\n    - Internal linking from guides to event detail pages and vice versa.\n  - Map these to existing or new GSD phases (e.g. a \"Guides\" phase in &#96;.planning/phases&#96;).\n\n- **6.3 Editorial cadence and workflow**\n  - Decide a sustainable cadence (e.g. one new guide or major update every 2–3 weeks through the summer).\n  - Define a simple checklist for creating/updating a guide:\n    - Choose theme \u0026 time window.\n    - Run pipeline, import new events.\n    - Curate 10–30 standout events for the guide.\n    - Write intro + 2–3 tips.\n    - Publish and promote (newsletter, social, etc.).\n\n---\n\n### 7. Data \u0026 Pipeline Alignment with UX\n\n- **7.1 Category mapping clean-up**\n  - Expand &#96;CATEGORY_MAP&#96; in &#96;category_mapping.py&#96; to cover all major upstream strings you care about (Sports / Soccer / Soccer, LGBTQ+, Karaoke, etc.), mapping them into your 17 canonical categories.\n  - Keep a small, human-readable table of mappings for future maintenance.\n\n- **7.2 Venue naming rules**\n  - Finalize rules for how scrapers write &#96;Event Venue Name&#96; so they always match canonical TEC venues (and never embed address data in the name).\n  - If new venues appear, fold them into your venue CSV + TEC venue importer before using them heavily in events.\n\n- **7.3 Operational runbook**\n  - Write a short runbook for yourself describing:\n    - How often to run &#96;./run_all_with_audit_v2.command&#96;.\n    - How to import &#96;ready_to_import_v2/ready_to_import_from_audit_UNIQUE_YYYY-MM-DD.csv&#96; into staging and then live.\n    - Spot-check steps (categories, venues, a few detail pages).\n\n---\n\n### 8. Metrics \u0026 Iteration Loop\n\n- **8.1 Define key metrics**\n  - For UX:\n    - Views of &#96;/tonight&#96; and &#96;/this-weekend&#96;.\n    - Clicks from event cards to external ticket/venue sites.\n  - For content:\n    - Views and time on guide pages.\n    - Distribution of events across key categories (Live Music, Comedy, etc.).\n\n- **8.2 Simple feedback mechanism**\n  - Add one low-friction way for users to give feedback (e.g. a small link: \"Missing something? Tell us.\").\n\n- **8.3 Monthly review ritual**\n  - Once a month, review:\n    - Metrics.\n    - Category/venue anomalies.\n    - UX friction points.\n  - Decide 1–2 changes to ship the following month, keeping scope small but consistent.\n\n---\n\n### How this fits your existing GSD roadmap\n\n- Use this plan as a **front-end/content layer** on top of your existing pipeline/stabilization phases.\n- Translate major chunks (e.g. \"Guides \u0026 Summer Content\", \"Category/venue mapping clean-up\", \"Visual design system\") into new or updated phases in &#96;.planning/ROADMAP.md&#96; so that the GSD agents (and you) can treat them as first-class work items.\n- Continue using staging as your proving ground for all UX/content experiments, then promote those changes to live once they feel right.\n",
```

Here’s a concise “Direction Brief” based on your roadmap and GSD docs, plus the next UX/IA step.

### Halifax Now – Direction Brief (from your roadmaps)

- **What it is**:  
  A Halifax‑focused event discovery site that aggregates events from 20+ venues/sources (including hard‑to‑find Instagram‑style content) into a single, searchable calendar powered by your Python scraper ecosystem and WordPress + The Events Calendar.

- **Current technical state**:  
  - ~27 scrapers registered, ~23 active.  
  - Stable merge + V2 audit pipeline producing `master_events.csv` and import‑ready CSVs.  
  - WordPress/TEC rebuilt with clean categories and venues, `/events`, `/tonight`, `/this-weekend` working, and a staging→live flow in place.  
  - Some scrapers still need tuning (Discover Halifax, Comedy Fest, a few category mappings), but the core system is healthy.

- **Near‑term product intent**:  
  - Focus on **user experience and content** now that pipeline is stable (your roadmap explicitly deferred design/SEO until “post‑scraper stabilisation”).  
  - Lean hard into **“Tonight”**, **“This Weekend”**, and **neighbourhood/category browsing** as the site’s differentiators.  
  - Start building a **summer content layer** (guides, themed pages) on top of the raw events.

- **Big bets for the next 6–12 months**:  
  - Make Halifax Now the default answer for “What’s on in Halifax tonight/this weekend?”  
  - Use your unique data coverage (vs editorial‑only competitors) to power strong SEO and curated guides.  
  - Gradually enrich recurring venues (Bus Stop, The Carleton, Bearly’s, etc.) with social/Instagram context once the core UX is solid.

---

### Next: UX principles & IA (navigation) proposal

Based on that direction, here’s a concrete set of UX principles and navigation/IA decisions (this completes the second todo conceptually):

- **UX principles for Halifax Now**
  - **Tonight first**: “What can I do *tonight*?” is answered within 1–2 clicks from anywhere.
  - **Glanceable cards**: Each event card must be readable in under 3 seconds: clear title, when, where, what‑type, price.
  - **Neighbourhood‑aware**: It’s easy to narrow to Downtown / North End / Dartmouth etc. on all key views.
  - **Low friction to source**: When someone decides “yes”, the ticket/venue link is obvious and near the top.
  - **No dead ends**: Every event detail page offers at least 3 related events (same venue, category, or date).
  - **Staging first**: All UX and content changes are proven on staging before touching live.

- **Core navigation & IA (initial pass)**
  - **Top nav items**:  
    - `Tonight` → `/tonight` (list view, today only).  
    - `This Weekend` → `/this-weekend`.  
    - `All Events` → `/events` (default list).  
    - `Categories` → index page listing main categories (links to TEC category archives).  
    - `Neighbourhoods` → index page with links like Downtown, North End, Dartmouth, etc. (can start simple).  
    - (Later: `Guides` when summer guides are live.)
  - **Key pages / flows**:
    - `/events` – master calendar with filters (Category, Date Range, Neighbourhood, Venue).
    - `/tonight` – today’s events, using your shortcode and list view.
    - `/this-weekend` – Fri–Sun via `[hfxnow_this_weekend]`.
    - Category hubs via TEC category archives (e.g. `/event-category/live-music/`).
    - Neighbourhood hubs (can be TEC category or a simple WP page + filtered TEC shortcode).
    - Future guides as regular WP pages that embed filtered TEC views plus editorial text.
  - **IA priorities for summer**:
    - Ensure `/tonight` and `/this-weekend` feel first‑class (in nav and in internal links).  
    - Make sure every event page links “up” to its category and venue (for easy lateral exploration).  
    - Prepare placeholders for 2–3 summer guide pages under a `Guides` section.

If you’re happy with this brief + UX/IA framing, next I’ll:

- Propose concrete **card and detail layouts** (what we’ll actually implement in TEC templates/CSS).
- Sketch a **summer content plan** (specific guide pages and cadence).
- Then specify the **category/venue pipeline tweaks** needed to support that UX cleanly.

---

### Next planning layers we should add

Given the Direction Brief and high‑level roadmap, the most useful next step is to specify **concrete designs and flows** you can implement later without re‑thinking everything. I’ll outline these as decisions/specs, not code.

---

### 1. Event card specification (list + featured)

**1.1 Compact list card (`/events`, `/tonight`, `/this-weekend`)**

For each event card, in order:

1. **Category label**  
   - Single canonical category (from your 17), e.g. “Live Music”, “Comedy”, “Family & Kids”.  
   - Style: small pill or subtle label above the title.

2. **Title**  
   - Single line (truncate with ellipsis on overflow).  
   - Clickable, goes to event detail page.

3. **Date & time**  
   - Human format, e.g.  
     - “Mon, Mar 23 · 7:00–9:30 pm” for range, or  
     - “Tue, Mar 17 · 8:00 pm”.  
   - If multi‑day, show “Mar 16–20” under the day name.

4. **Venue + neighbourhood**  
   - “Good Robot Brewing Co. · North End Halifax”.  
   - If neighbourhood is unknown, just show venue.

5. **Price indicator**  
   - “Free”, “From $20”, or “$20–$35”.  
   - If price is missing, show “See website” or nothing (decide one rule and stick to it).

6. **Short description**  
   - 1–2 lines from the description, clamped.

7. **Optional badges**  
   - Small labels like “Tonight”, “New”, “Selling fast” if you decide to add them later.

**1.2 Featured card (for guides/homepage)**

Same fields, but:

- Larger image on top (cropped 16:9), then:
  - Category pill
  - Title (2 lines max)
  - Date/time
  - Venue + neighbourhood
  - 1–3 line description
  - Price indicator + subtle “Get tickets” link

This becomes your building block for summer guides like “Patios” or “Summer festivals”.

---

### 2. Event detail page spec

Above the fold:

1. **Title**  
2. **Primary meta block**:
   - Date & time (including multi‑day info).  
   - Venue name (linking to a venue page/TEC venue archive).  
   - Category (linking to category archive).  
   - Price summary.

3. **Primary CTA**  
   - “Get tickets” or “Visit website”, using the `Event URL` from your CSV.  
   - Open in new tab.

Below the fold:

- **Description** – full or near‑full body text.
- **Venue section** – brief address, map (TEC’s venue block or similar), and link to “More events at [Venue]”.
- **Tags / themes** – optional; useful later for guides.
- **Related events** – e.g.:
  - 3 events from the same venue, OR  
  - 3 events from the same category within the next 30 days.

Constraints:

- On mobile, keep the CTA visible near the top; don’t make people scroll long descriptions before seeing where to buy/learn more.

---

### 3. Navigation & IA specifics

**3.1 Top navigation (live site)**

- `Tonight` → `/tonight` (TEC list view via shortcode).
- `This Weekend` → `/this-weekend`.
- `All Events` → `/events`.
- `Event Categories` → a simple WP page listing links to key TEC category archives (Live Music, Comedy, Arts & Culture, Family & Kids, Food & Drink, Sports, etc.).
- `Neighbourhoods` → a simple WP page listing neighbourhood entry points (can initially just link to filtered `/events` URLs or static pages with a TEC shortcode).

**3.2 Future `Guides` section**

- One WP page `/guides/` that lists:
  - “Summer Festivals 2026”
  - “Patio & Waterfront Guide”
  - “Family Summer in Halifax”
  - etc.

Each guide is:

- A standard page with:
  - Intro paragraph,
  - 2–3 “tip” bullets,
  - A TEC shortcode filtered by category/tags/date, plus a few **featured cards** curated manually on top if you want extra control.

---

### 4. Summer content plan (high‑level)

Given your timelines and capacity, a realistic plan:

- **April–May**:
  - Guide 1: **“Spring & Summer Festivals”**
  - Guide 2: **“Patio & Waterfront Events”**

- **June–July**:
  - Guide 3: **“Family Summer”**
  - Guide 4: **“Neighbourhood Nights: North End & Downtown”**

Each guide uses:

- Your pipeline CSVs to source events (filtered by category & date).
- A manually curated intro + 10–20 “editor’s picks”.

---

### 5. Pipeline adjustments to support this UX

We’ll need, when you’re ready to implement:

- **Category mapping tweaks**  
  - Make sure values like `Sports / Soccer / Soccer`, `Karaoke`, `LGBTQ+` are mapped into:
    - Sports
    - Games & Trivia
    - Live Music or Community & Charity (decide exactly once).
- **Venue naming**  
  - Ensure `Event Venue Name` in the CSV:
    - Uses canonical venue titles (no addresses in the name),
    - Matches the venue posts created in TEC.

Those can be implemented later with small, targeted edits to `category_mapping.py`, `venue_aliases.py`, and the relevant scrapers.

---

### 6. Immediate planning next step

To keep momentum but stay in planning mode:

- We can now:
  - Pick **which 2 summer guides** you want to start with, and
  - Outline their exact structure (sections, how to pull events, any special rules like “only free events” or “only patios within X km of downtown”).

If you tell me which 2 guide ideas excite you most for this summer, I’ll draft those two in detail next (structure, content types, and how they hook into your data and TEC views).

### 1. Venue Registry & Recovery (using `venue_aliases.py`)

**Goal**: Rebuild and extend your canonical venue set, then align pipeline + TEC to it so venues aren’t “lost” again.

**Plan:**

- **1.1 Extract existing canonical venues**
  - Use `[halifax_event_scrapers_v3/venue_aliases.py](/Users/katespurr/Desktop/halifax_event_scrapers_v3/venue_aliases.py)` as the starting “source of truth”:
    - Canonical keys in `VENUE_ALIASES` (e.g. `"light house arts centre"`, `"the carleton"`, `"bearly's house of blues and ribs"`, etc.).
  - Convert those canonical keys into human‑readable display names (title‑cased, with proper accents), e.g.:
    - `light house arts centre` → `Light House Arts Centre`
    - `bearly's house of blues and ribs` → `Bearly’s House of Blues and Ribs`.

- **1.2 Build a canonical venue list**
  - Create a conceptual list (later a file, e.g. `venues.csv` or `venues.yaml`) with columns/fields:
    - Display name
    - Canonical key (lowercase internal key from `venue_aliases.py`)
    - Address, city, province, country
    - Neighbourhood
  - Extend this list with:
    - Any obvious missing venues you name (beyond the original 24).
    - Venues inferred from current events on live (scraped venue names you trust).

- **1.3 Re‑import venues into TEC**
  - Generate a **new venue CSV** from that canonical list.
  - On staging (later in execution mode), re‑import via TEC Venues importer:
    - Ensure each row creates/updates a `tribe_venue` post with the correct display name + address.
  - Once satisfied, repeat on live.

- **1.4 Align pipeline to venue registry**
  - Confirm that:
    - `normalize_venue()` + `get_venue_display_name()` always return keys that exist in the registry.
    - `Event Venue Name` in the TEC CSV is always the **display name** from the registry (not raw scraped names / addresses).
  - This makes venue linking reliable and prevents future deletions from “losing” names in your data layer.

---

### 2. Category‑Specific Placeholder Images

**Goal**: Every event has a visually consistent image, with category‑specific placeholders where needed.

**Plan:**

- **2.1 Define placeholder set**
  - Create a small set of image placeholders:
    - `live-music-placeholder` (e.g. stylized guitar or sound wave)
    - `comedy-placeholder`
    - `arts-culture-placeholder`
    - `family-kids-placeholder`
    - `sports-placeholder`
    - `food-drink-placeholder`
    - `default-placeholder` for all others / “Other”
  - Keep them simple, flat, and recognizably “Halifax Now” (colors/fonts from your design tokens).

- **2.2 Mapping logic**
  - For each canonical category, define which placeholder to use, e.g.:
    - Live Music → live‑music placeholder
    - Comedy, Games & Trivia → comedy placeholder
    - Arts & Culture, Film & Cinema, Theatre & Performance → arts‑culture placeholder
    - Family & Kids → family placeholder
    - Food & Drink → food‑drink placeholder
    - Sports, Outdoors & Nature → sports placeholder
    - Everything else → default placeholder.

- **2.3 Where to apply**
  - Decide implementation approach for later:
    - **Pipeline approach**: in `merge_master_events.py`, if `Event Featured Image` is blank, set it to the placeholder URL based on category.
    - **Template approach**: in TEC templates, if no featured image is set, render the placeholder instead (less intrusive to data).
  - For now in planning, we treat this as a view concern: the CSV may stay blank; the presentation layer will supply the correct fallback.

---

### 3. Hiding Event End Time on Listings

**Goal**: List views show clean, simple times (start only), while detail pages keep full information.

**Plan:**

- **3.1 Listing rules**
  - On `/events`, `/tonight`, `/this-weekend`, category archives, and any list views:
    - Show **only**:
      - Date (e.g. “Tue, Mar 17”).
      - Start time (e.g. “7:00 pm”).
    - Hide end time entirely, except for:
      - All‑day or clearly multi‑day events, where a short “Mar 16–20” style is okay.

- **3.2 Detail page rules**
  - Keep full start/end date and time on the event detail page, e.g.:
    - “Tuesday, March 17, 2026 · 7:00–9:30 pm”
    - “March 16–20, 2026 · Daily”.

- **3.3 Implementation direction**
  - When we move to execution:
    - Prefer a **TEC template override** for list/card views (alter the markup to omit end time).
    - Keep TEC settings as‑is where possible, so we don’t fight updates.

---

We’re essentially adding the following under your current plan:

### 1. Venue registry generator (for rebuilding venues)

In `halifax_event_scrapers_v3` I added a helper script:

```12:88:/Users/katespurr/Desktop/halifax_event_scrapers_v3/generate_venue_registry.py
"""
Generate a canonical venue registry CSV for Halifax Now.
...
Output:
    output/venue_registry.csv
"""
...
if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

What it does:

- Reads any existing `output/venue_import.csv` (your original 24‑venue import).
- Reads all canonical venue keys from `venue_aliases.VENUE_ALIASES`.
- Produces **`output/venue_registry.csv`** with columns:

  - `display_name`
  - `canonical_key`
  - `address`
  - `city`
  - `province`
  - `country`
  - `neighbourhood`

How to use it (when you’re ready to run commands):

1. From your Mac terminal:

   ```bash
   cd ~/Desktop/halifax_event_scrapers_v3
   python3 generate_venue_registry.py
   ```

2. Open `output/venue_registry.csv` in a spreadsheet.
3. Fill in missing address / city / province / country / neighbourhood.
4. Import that file via **Events → Import → Venues** on **staging first**, then live.

This gives you a canonical venue list you won’t “lose” again and that we can keep in sync with the scrapers and TEC.

### Live Music Placeholder – Design Brief

**Goal**: A reusable 16:9 image that clearly says “Live Music in Halifax” without clashing with event photos. It should look good as a card thumbnail and on the event detail page.

---

### 1. Canvas & Layout

- **Aspect ratio**: 16:9 (e.g. 1200×675 px or 1600×900 px).
- **Safe area**: Keep key elements within the central 80% of width and height so nothing important gets cropped on different screens.

**Layout (left → right):**

- **Left third**: main icon (guitar neck or mic).
- **Right two‑thirds**: abstract sound waves and a very simple skyline hint.

---

### 2. Colors

Use a dark, night‑out palette with a bright accent:

- **Background**: deep navy
  - Example: `#0A1B2A`
- **Primary accent** (sound waves / highlights):
  - Bright aqua: `#18C3C8`
- **Secondary accent** (details on the icon):
  - Warm coral or orange: `#FF7A59`
- **Neutral for outlines**:
  - Off‑white: `#F5F7FA`

Constraint:

- Avoid too many different colors; 3–4 total is ideal so the placeholder feels cohesive and doesn’t fight with the rest of your UI.

---

### 3. Main Icon (Left Third)

Pick **either** guitar neck or microphone; both are fine, but don’t combine too many elements.

**Option A – Guitar neck (recommended)**

- Show the **top half** of a vertical guitar neck starting from the bottom edge and reaching about 60–70% of the height.
- No full guitar body, just:
  - Neck with 4–6 frets and simple fret markers.
  - Headstock with 3–4 tuning pegs (simplified shapes).
- Color:
  - Neck fill: dark teal (`#123B4D`) slightly lighter than the background.
  - Fret markers and strings: off‑white (`#F5F7FA`).
  - Tiny accent details (e.g. headstock edge) in coral (`#FF7A59`).

**Option B – Microphone on stand**

- Classic cardioid mic on a simple stand.
- Slight tilt toward the right.
- Spotlight suggestion (see below) for context.

Either way, keep shapes flat / vector‑style, with no photo textures.

---

### 4. Sound Waves & Background Elements (Right Side)

- From the middle area of the canvas, draw **3–4 smooth, horizontal wave lines** flowing left→right:
  - Use the bright aqua `#18C3C8`.
  - Vary line thickness slightly (e.g. 3px, 5px, 4px) for visual interest.
  - They should not intersect the text safe area too aggressively; keep them primarily in the top/mid third.

- Behind the waves, add a very minimal **skyline hint** along the bottom:
  - Simple rectangles for buildings: 3–5 rectangles of varying heights.
  - Fill color: a very dark version of the background (e.g. `#050D15`) so they’re barely visible—more “texture” than focal point.
  - Optionally, one tiny triangle to hint at a clocktower or recognizable building, but keep it abstract.

---

### 5. Optional Text

You can include a small label, but it should not dominate:

- Text: `Live Music`
- Font: simple, sans‑serif that matches your site (e.g. Inter / system UI).
- Size: small enough that it’s readable but not the main element (maybe ~24–32 px on a 1200×675 canvas).
- Placement:
  - Bottom‑right corner, with 8–12 px padding from edges.
- Color:
  - Off‑white text on a subtle, semi‑transparent dark pill behind it:
    - Background: `rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.35)` with 8 px border radius.

You can also choose to **skip the text** and let the category label in the UI provide the wording; the graphic alone (guitar + waves) is strong enough.

---

### 6. Edge Cases & Reuse

- **Scaling**: Verify it still reads clearly when shrunk to ~300 px wide (typical card thumbnail).
- **Cropping**: Assume some vertical cropping on small screens; ensure:
  - Guitar neck / mic is not too close to top/bottom edge.
  - The key action (strings, mic head) stays visible even if top/bottom 10% are cut off.

- **Future flexibility**:
  - If you later create a dark theme or adjust brand colors, this placeholder should still work by simply swapping the background and accent hex values.

---

If you’d like, next I can do the same level of detail for **Comedy** or **Food & Drink**, or help you translate this brief into a concrete asset checklist (e.g. “create in Canva with these exact settings, export at X size, then upload here”).

---

_**Agent (default)**_

Totally doable to avoid per‑event image wrangling in Canva; we just need to be clear what “no Canva” means.

### What CSS can and can’t do here

- **Can do**:
  - Use **one or a few static assets** (SVG/PNG) and apply them automatically with CSS based on category.
  - Use **pure CSS shapes/gradients** (no image file) as very simple placeholders (e.g. colored blocks, minimal icons via webfonts).
- **Can’t do**:
  - Magically generate rich illustrations with no assets at all; you either:
    - Have 1–N generic assets reused everywhere, or
    - Use very simple, geometric CSS‑only placeholders.

Given you don’t want to touch Canva for imports, the sweet spot is:

- **1–N category‑specific assets you create once**,  
- Then **pure logic + CSS** decides when/where they appear.

### Option A – Category placeholders driven purely by CSS

Assuming TEC adds category classes to event cards (it does, e.g. `.tribe-events-category-live-music`):

1. **Upload a handful of placeholder images once** to WordPress media:
   - `live-music-placeholder.png`
   - `comedy-placeholder.png`
   - etc.

2. **Use CSS to show them only when no featured image is set**. Conceptually:

```css
/* Generic placeholder when image missing */
.tribe-events-calendar-list__event .tribe-events-event-image {
  background-size: cover;
  background-position: center;
}

/* Example: Live Music category */
.tribe-events-category-live-music .tribe-events-event-image:not(:has(img)) {
  background-image: url('https://halifax-now.ca/wp-content/uploads/.../live-music-placeholder.png');
}

/* Comedy */
.tribe-events-category-comedy .tribe-events-event-image:not(:has(img)) {
  background-image: url('https://.../comedy-placeholder.png');
}
```

- You don’t touch individual events or imports at all.
- Once you have the few assets, this is **100% automatic**.

(If `:has()` support is an issue in your browser matrix, there are template‑based fallbacks, but the idea is the same: only show the background when there’s no `<img>`.)

### Option B – Pure CSS shapes (no image files)

If you want **zero** graphic files:

- Use a solid background color + a simple icon font or emoji:

```css
.tribe-events-calendar-list__event .tribe-events-event-image:not(:has(img)) {
  background: linear-gradient(145deg, #0A1B2A, #123B4D);
  display: flex;
  align-items: center;
  justify-content: center;
  color: #F5F7FA;
  font-size: 2.4rem;
}

/* Live Music: musical note */
.tribe-events-category-live-music .tribe-events-event-image:not(:has(img))::before {
  content: "♪";
}

/* Comedy: smiley */
.tribe-events-category-comedy .tribe-events-event-image:not(:has(img))::before {
  content: "😄";
}
```

It’s less “designed” but requires **no external tooling** and still looks intentional.

---

If you confirm which of these you prefer (A: a few simple PNG/SVGs you create once, or B: pure CSS gradients + icons/emojis), I can outline the exact selectors you’d use with The Events Calendar on your site so implementation is just paste‑and‑tweak later.

Yes, this is very workable—and it’s a strong layout.

Here’s a concrete mapping plan from this HTML to your actual WordPress + TEC setup, broken into practical phases you can implement later.

---

### 1. Navigation Bar

**What you have in the HTML**

- Sticky nav with logo lockup (`.nav-logo`, `.logo-name`, `.logo-tag`) and menu links in `.nav-links`.
- CTA button style for a special link (e.g. “Guides” or “Newsletter”).

**How to implement in WP**

- **Menu items**:
  - Create a WP menu with:
    - `Tonight` → `/tonight`
    - `This Weekend` → `/this-weekend`
    - `All Events` → `/events`
    - `Categories` → categories page
    - `Neighbourhoods` → neighbourhood hub
    - Optional CTA: `Guides` or `Newsletter`.
- **Styling**:
  - Port the `nav`, `.nav-logo`, `.logo-name`, `.logo-tag`, `.nav-links`, `.nav-links li a`, and `.nav-links li.cta a` CSS into:
    - Your child theme’s stylesheet, or
    - Additional CSS in the customizer/editor.
- **Logo**:
  - Implement the lockup using a simple SVG or image + text (you can approximate the HTML structure with a block pattern or a small header template override).

---

### 2. Hero Section

**What you have**

- Left side:
  - Eyebrow (`.hero-eyebrow`), big H1, short paragraph (`.hero-desc`), search bar (`.search-bar`).
- Right side:
  - “Featured Picks” label (`.picks-label`) and 3 stacked `.pick-card` entries (category, title, meta, small tag).

**How to implement**

- **Where**: on the **homepage** (or top of `/events` if you make that your main landing).
- **Left column**:
  - Build a block pattern or small template part with:
    - Static eyebrow (“Tonight in Halifax” or similar).
    - H1 like “What’s on in Halifax”.
    - Short description (one or two sentences).
    - Search bar wired to TEC:
      - Use TEC’s event search form or a shortcode/HTML that submits to `/events` with the right query parameter.
- **Right column**:
  - Initially: 3 manual “editor’s picks”:
    - Use a Group block with:
      - Category label (text).
      - Event title linked to the TEC event.
      - Date/time + venue line.
      - Small tag like “Tonight” or “Just added”.
  - Later: you can automate this by:
    - Tagging events as `featured` and using a TEC shortcode filtered by tag, then styling its output to match `.pick-card`.

---

### 3. Filter Bar (Chips)

**What you have**

- Horizontal chip row `.filter-bar` with label (`.filter-bar-label`) and clickable chips `.chip` (with an `.on` state).

**How to implement**

- **Purpose**: visual shell for TEC’s filter bar or custom links.
- Two options:
  - **Wrap TEC’s native filters** in this styling so they look like chips.  
  - Or create a **custom chip row** that links to filtered `/events` URLs (e.g. `?tribe_eventcategory=live-music`, `?price=free`).

- **Recommended first step**:
  - Above `/events` list view, add a custom block that renders:
    - “Filter by” label,
    - A chip for each major category (Live Music, Comedy, Arts & Culture, etc.),
    - Each chip is just a link to the category archive or a pre‑filtered view (e.g. `/events/?tribe_eventcategory=live-music`).
  - Apply `.filter-bar`, `.filter-bar-label`, `.chip` CSS from the HTML to this block.

---

### 4. Event Grid & List Components

The HTML gives you two strong patterns:

- `.event-grid` / `.ec` (3‑column grid with one wide feature).
- `.event-list` / `.eli` (list rows with a date badge and meta).

**How to map them**

- **Primary list view (`/events`, `/tonight`, `/this-weekend`)**:
  - Use the **list pattern** (`.event-list`, `.eli`):
    - Left: date badge (`.eli-date`, `.eli-day`, `.eli-mon`).
    - Middle: title, category, meta (time, venue, neighbourhood).
    - Right: price/badge.
  - This is the pattern you’d implement by:
    - Overriding TEC’s list template to output the same structure.
    - Applying `.event-list`, `.eli` CSS.

- **Grid usage**:
  - Reserve the **grid pattern** (`.event-grid`, `.ec`) for:
    - Guides (e.g. “Summer Festivals”) and more editorial sections on the homepage.
    - Category hubs where you want a more “magazine” feel.
  - Implementation:
    - Use TEC shortcodes to pull events into a custom page.
    - Wrap them in a custom template or block that emits markup compatible with `.event-grid` / `.ec`.

---

### 5. Section Headers & Toggles

The CSS includes:

- `.sec-head`, `.sec-title`, `.sec-link` – section headers with a right‑aligned “See all” link.
- `.view-toggle`, `.vt` – a simple “List / Grid” toggle.

**How to use them**

- **Section headers**:
  - Use them on the homepage and guides:
    - “Tonight in Halifax” section with a `See all` link to `/tonight`.
    - “This Weekend” section with `See all` to `/this-weekend`.
    - “Live Music” section linking to `Live Music` category archive.
  - Implement using a reusable block pattern:
    - H2 with `.sec-title`,
    - Right‑aligned link with `.sec-link`.

- **View toggle**:
  - This is nice to have, but optional initially.
  - Later you could:
    - Add a small UI above `/events` to switch between list/grid views.
    - For v1, you can keep `/events` as list and use grids only on curated/guides pages.

---

### 6. How to phase this in without breaking the site

1. **Phase 1 – Navigation & hero** (low risk)
   - Implement the new nav bar and hero (left + right) on the homepage.
   - Keep TEC views unchanged while you verify the look and feel.

2. **Phase 2 – Filter chips + Tonight/Weekend sections**
   - Add the chip bar and Tonight/This Weekend sections using TEC shortcodes and the `.filter-bar` / `.sec-head` styles.
   - Confirm navigation between these views feels smooth.

3. **Phase 3 – Event list template**
   - Override TEC’s list view to match the `.event-list` / `.eli` pattern:
     - Date badge left,
     - Metadata middle,
     - Price badge right.
   - Test `/events`, `/tonight`, `/this-weekend` on staging before pushing to live.

4. **Phase 4 – Grids and guides**
   - As you roll out summer guides, use the `.event-grid` / `.ec` pattern for those curated pages.

---

If you’d like, next I can draft an **exact implementation checklist just for Phase 1** (nav + hero), including which parts of this CSS to copy first and how to approximate the HTML structure with Gutenberg blocks / theme templates in your current WordPress setup.

### Phase 1 – Nav + Hero Implementation Checklist (WordPress + TEC)

This keeps risk low: only header + top of homepage, no TEC template overrides yet.

---

### 1. Navigation Bar

**1.1 Create the menu**

In WP admin on live (or staging first):

- Go to **Appearance → Menus** (or “Editor → Navigation” if using Site Editor).
- Create / edit your main navigation with links:

  - `Tonight` → `/tonight`
  - `This Weekend` → `/this-weekend`
  - `All Events` → `/events`
  - `Event Categories` → categories page (can be a simple new page for now)
  - `Neighbourhoods` → neighbourhood hub page
  - Optional CTA item (e.g. `Guides` or `Newsletter`).

**1.2 Structure the header**

Using Site Editor (Twenty Twenty‑Five) or a header template:

- Put a **Group** block for the header with:
  - Left:  
    - Optional logo image block or small SVG icon.  
    - Two stacked text blocks:
      - Site name text styled like `.logo-name` (e.g. “Halifax Now”).
      - Tiny tagline text styled like `.logo-tag` (e.g. “What to do tonight”).
  - Right:  
    - Your Navigation block with the menu you just created.

You don’t have to perfectly mirror the HTML; focus on: logo left, menu right.

**1.3 Add the nav CSS**

In **Appearance → Customize → Additional CSS** or Site Editor “Additional CSS”:

- Copy in only the nav styles you need from the HTML (adjust selectors to match your theme if needed):

```css
nav.site-header, header.wp-block-template-part {
  position: sticky;
  top: 0;
  z-index: 200;
  background: #FFFFFF;
  border-bottom: 1.5px solid #080808;
}

/* Logo text styles */
.logo-name {
  font-family: 'Barlow Condensed', system-ui, sans-serif;
  font-weight: 900;
  font-size: 21px;
  letter-spacing: 0.03em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  color: #1A0FCC;
}

.logo-tag {
  font-family: 'Barlow Condensed', system-ui, sans-serif;
  font-weight: 700;
  font-size: 9.5px;
  letter-spacing: 0.16em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  color: #1A0FCC;
  opacity: 0.55;
  margin-top: 2px;
}

/* Menu links */
.wp-block-navigation a {
  font-family: 'Barlow Condensed', system-ui, sans-serif;
  font-weight: 700;
  font-size: 13px;
  letter-spacing: 0.09em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  color: #080808;
  text-decoration: none;
  padding: 0 16px;
}

.wp-block-navigation a:hover {
  color: #1A0FCC;
}
```

(You can refine selectors once you see how your theme outputs markup.)

---

### 2. Homepage Hero (top of front page)

Assuming you set a **static front page**:

**2.1 Set a static front page (if not already)**

- Go to **Settings → Reading**:
  - “Your homepage displays” → “A static page”.
  - Select a page called “Home” or create one.

**2.2 Build the hero block layout**

Edit that “Home” page in the block editor:

- Add a **Group** (full‑width, background color):

  - Set background color to your blue (`#1A0FCC`).
  - Set text color to white.
  - Add padding (e.g. 60px top, 40px sides, 52px bottom).

- Inside, add a **Columns (2)** block with:

  - **Left column**:
    - Small text (eyebrow): “WHAT’S ON IN HALIFAX” (uppercase).
    - H1: something like “Find something fun tonight”.
    - Short paragraph: 1–2 sentences about the site.
    - A **Row** with:
      - Search input (use a simple Search block or TEC’s event search shortcode `[tribe_events view="list" search=""]` wired into your events page).
      - Button labeled “Search”.

  - **Right column**:
    - Text block: “EDITOR’S PICKS”.
    - Below it, 3 stacked **Group** blocks as “pick cards”:
      - Each contains:
        - Small category label (e.g. “Live Music”).
        - Event title (link to the event).
        - Tiny meta line with date/time + venue.
        - Optional small badge like “Tonight” or “Free”.

You can always start with 1–2 picks and add more later.

**2.3 Add hero CSS for polish**

In Additional CSS, bring over and simplify just the basics:

```css
/* Hero container */
.home-hero {
  background: #1A0FCC;
  color: #FFFFFF;
  padding: 60px 40px 52px;
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: minmax(0, 1.1fr) minmax(0, 1fr);
  gap: 56px;
  align-items: center;
}

/* Eyebrow */
.home-hero .hero-eyebrow {
  font-family: 'Barlow Condensed', system-ui, sans-serif;
  font-weight: 700;
  font-size: 11px;
  letter-spacing: 0.2em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  opacity: 0.5;
  margin-bottom: 16px;
}

/* Main heading */
.home-hero h1 {
  font-family: 'Barlow Condensed', system-ui, sans-serif;
  font-weight: 900;
  font-size: clamp(42px, 6vw, 84px);
  line-height: 0.9;
  letter-spacing: -0.01em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  margin-bottom: 22px;
}

/* Description */
.home-hero .hero-desc {
  font-size: 15px;
  opacity: 0.75;
  max-width: 360px;
  line-height: 1.65;
  margin-bottom: 28px;
}

/* Picks label + cards (right column) */
.home-hero .picks-label {
  font-family: 'Barlow Condensed', system-ui, sans-serif;
  font-weight: 700;
  font-size: 10px;
  letter-spacing: 0.18em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  opacity: 0.5;
  margin-bottom: 10px;
}

.home-hero .pick-card {
  padding: 14px 16px;
  border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.15);
  border-bottom: none;
  display: flex;
  justify-content: space-between;
  gap: 12px;
  cursor: pointer;
}

.home-hero .pick-card:last-child {
  border-bottom: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.15);
}
```

- Wrap the hero block in a Group with an extra class (e.g. `home-hero`) via the block settings → “Advanced → Additional CSS class”.

---

### 3. Quick checks after Phase 1

Once nav + hero are in place:

- On **desktop** and **mobile**:
  - Nav stays sticky, links work, logo looks reasonable.
  - Hero text is legible, layout doesn’t break on narrow screens.
  - The search bar submits to `/events` (or wherever you point it) and shows filtered results.

- TEC views (`/events`, `/tonight`, `/this-weekend`) still work as before (we haven’t touched their templates yet).

---

When you’re happy with this, Phase 2 would be:

- Adding the **chip‑style filter bar** above `/events`.
- Adding **Tonight/This Weekend sections** to the homepage using TEC shortcodes, styled with the `.sec-head` pattern.

We can plan that next once you’ve had a chance to see Phase 1 live.

Yes, they can do both - they just need to keep them separate so they can compare. A few options:

1. **Staging vs live**: Try Claude's child theme on one environment (e.g. staging) and my block + CSS approach on the other (e.g. live), then compare.

2. **Same site, different pages**: Use the child theme for some pages/templates and the block approach for others - but that could get messy.

3. **Same site, switch between**: Keep both sets of files/code but only activate one at a time - e.g. activate Claude's child theme when they want to see that version, then switch to a different theme or remove child theme and use my approach. WordPress only allows one theme (and one child) to be active at once, so "both" means having two ways to achieve the same thing and choosing which to use, or testing on two different sites.

4. **Merge the best of both**: Take the child theme Claude made (which might have template overrides, custom header, etc.) and layer in my checklist (e.g. the specific CSS variables, the hero structure) into that child theme. _**Agent (default)**_


Yes, you can do both. Easiest is to **treat Claude’s child theme as the base** and add the “my way” pieces into it so you have one coherent setup.

### Option A – Merge into the child theme (recommended)

- **Keep** Claude’s child theme active (that’s your layout/structure).
- **Add** the Phase 1 checklist inside that child theme:
  - In the child theme’s `style.css` (or a separate CSS file enqueued by the child), add the **nav + hero CSS** from the checklist (variables, `.logo-name`, `.logo-tag`, hero grid, pick cards, etc.).
  - On the **homepage**, if Claude didn’t already build the hero, add the **hero block layout** (two columns, eyebrow + H1 + description + search, right column “Editor’s Picks” + 3 pick cards) via the block editor.
- Result: one site, one theme (the child), with both Claude’s structure and the checklist styling/content.

### Option B – Compare two versions on two environments

- **Staging**: use Claude’s child theme as-is (no checklist changes).
- **Live** (or a second staging install): use a different theme or a copy of the child theme and apply **only** the checklist (blocks + Additional CSS, no child theme).
- Then you compare and later choose one approach or merge the best bits into the child theme.

### Option C – Two “modes” on the same site

- Keep the child theme but add a **customizer option or a small plugin** that switches between two CSS/block presets (e.g. “Claude layout” vs “Checklist layout”). More work and usually unnecessary unless you really want A/B testing.

**Practical suggestion:** do **Option A**. Use the child theme Claude gave you, and layer in the checklist (nav CSS, hero block structure, hero CSS) inside that child theme. You get both: Claude’s child theme plus the structured nav + hero from the checklist. If you tell me the child theme folder name (e.g. `halifax-now-child`), I can give you exact file paths and snippet locations for pasting the checklist CSS and where to add the hero blocks.

_**Agent (default)**_


Yes. To do it cleanly, we need to be clear what “the staging site” is in Git terms.

### Two common setups

1. **Scraper / pipeline repo** (e.g. `halifax_event_scrapers_v3`)  
   - This is the Python scrapers, merge scripts, GSD planning, etc.  
   - It can live in one GitHub repo and feed both staging and live.

2. **WordPress “site” repo** (themes, child theme, customizations)  
   - This is what actually runs on staging.halifax-now.ca: child theme, custom mu-plugins, maybe a bit of config.  
   - Staging and live often share the **same** repo; you just deploy that repo to both (or copy files).  
   - So “staging needs its own repo” usually means: **we want a repo that holds the WordPress site code**, and staging (and live) both use that repo.

I’m assuming you mean: **create a GitHub repo for the WordPress site code** (child theme + any customizations) so staging (and live) are version-controlled and you can deploy from one place. If you meant only the scraper project, the steps are the same; we just point the repo at that folder.

---

### 1. Create the repo on GitHub

1. Go to [github.com/new](https://github.com/new).
2. **Repository name**: e.g. `halifax-now-site` or `halifax-now-wordpress`.
3. **Description**: e.g. “WordPress theme and customizations for Halifax Now (staging + live).”
4. Choose **Public** (or Private if you prefer).
5. Do **not** add a README, .gitignore, or license yet (we’ll do that locally so we don’t get two roots).
6. Click **Create repository**.

---

### 2. Decide what’s in the repo (what folder to use)

You have two common choices:

- **Option A – Only the child theme (and maybe mu-plugins)**  
  - Repo contains only the parts you edit: e.g. `halifax-now-child/` and maybe `mu-plugins/` (or a copy).  
  - You’d create that folder on your machine, put the child theme and custom plugins in it, then `git init` inside that folder and push to the new repo.

- **Option B – Full WordPress (or a “deploy” copy)**  
  - Repo contains a full copy of `wp-content/themes`, `wp-content/mu-plugins`, etc., so you can clone and deploy to staging/live.  
  - Usually you’d `git init` in a folder that has only those parts (not the whole `wp-admin` or DB).

So: **where is the child theme (and any custom PHP) right now?**  
- Only on the server (staging/live)?  
- Or do you already have a local folder that mirrors “what’s on staging”?

---

### 3. If the child theme only exists on the server

1. **Download** from the server (e.g. via SFTP or Plesk File Manager):
   - `wp-content/themes/<child-theme-name>/` (the whole folder).
   - Any custom `wp-content/mu-plugins/*.php` you care about (e.g. `hfxnow-calendar-ui.php`).
2. **On your Mac**, create a folder for the site repo, e.g.:
   ```bash
   mkdir -p ~/Desktop/Halifax\ Now/halifax-now-site
   cd ~/Desktop/Halifax\ Now/halifax-now-site
   ```
3. Put the child theme folder and (if you want) mu-plugins inside it, e.g.:
   - `halifax-now-site/themes/halifax-now-child/`
   - `halifax-now-site/mu-plugins/hfxnow-calendar-ui.php`
   so the repo layout matches how it will be deployed (themes and mu-plugins at the top level or under a single root).

---

### 4. Initialize Git and push to GitHub

In the folder that will be the repo root (e.g. `halifax-now-site`):

```bash
cd /path/to/halifax-now-site
git init
echo ".DS_Store" >> .gitignore
echo "*.log" >> .gitignore
git add .
git commit -m "Initial commit: child theme and customizations for Halifax Now"
git branch -M main
git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/halifax-now-site.git
git push -u origin main
```

Replace `YOUR_USERNAME` with your GitHub username and `halifax-now-site` with the repo name you chose.

---

### 5. After that

- **Staging** (and live) can both use this repo: you (or your host) pull/clone this repo and copy `themes/` and `mu-plugins/` into the right places on each server.
- Any time you change the child theme or custom plugins, commit and push from your local folder, then pull on the server (or use a simple deploy script).

If you tell me (1) whether the repo is for “WordPress site code” or “scraper project,” and (2) where the child theme lives right now (only on server vs already in a folder on your Mac), I can give you exact commands and folder names for your setup (e.g. “run these in `halifax_event_scrapers_v3`” or “create `halifax-now-site` and run these”).

# Cursor Instructions — Halifax Now Child Theme CSS Fixes

## File to edit:
`wp-content/themes/halifax-now-child/style.css`

## What to do:
Add the following CSS blocks to the BOTTOM of the existing style.css file.

## FIX 1 — Nav link underlines and background box

The navigation links (HOME, THIS WEEKEND IN HALIFAX, TONIGHT IN HALIFAX) have
an unwanted underline and a white background box around them. Remove both.

```css
/* ─── FIX: Nav link underlines ─── */
.wp-block-navigation a,
.wp-block-navigation .wp-block-navigation-item__content,
.wp-block-pages-list__item__link {
    text-decoration: none !important;
    border: none !important;
    border-bottom: none !important;
    box-shadow: none !important;
}

/* ─── FIX: Nav container transparent background ─── */
.wp-block-navigation__container,
.wp-block-navigation__responsive-container,
.wp-block-navigation__responsive-container-content {
    background: transparent !important;
    background-color: transparent !important;
    border: none !important;
    box-shadow: none !important;
}
```

---

## FIX 2 — White gap above homepage hero

On the homepage there is a large white gap between the blue nav bar and the
blue hero section. This is caused by top margin/padding on the <main> element
in the Pages template. Remove it on the homepage only.

```css
/* ─── FIX: Remove white gap above hero on homepage ─── */
.home main.wp-block-group {
    margin-top: 0 !important;
}

.home .wp-block-group.alignfull.has-global-padding:first-of-type {
    padding-top: 0 !important;
}
```

---

## FIX 3 — Homepage hero and category bar full width

The blue hero box and cream category chip bar are constrained to a narrow
column instead of going edge-to-edge. Fix this.

```css
/* ─── FIX: Hero and category bar full width ─── */
.hn-hero {
    margin-left: calc(-1 * var(--wp--preset--spacing--50)) !important;
    margin-right: calc(-1 * var(--wp--preset--spacing--50)) !important;
}

.hn-cats {
    margin-left: calc(-1 * var(--wp--preset--spacing--50)) !important;
    margin-right: calc(-1 * var(--wp--preset--spacing--50)) !important;
}
```

---

## FIX 4 — Load Barlow fonts properly in functions.php

## File to edit:
`wp-content/themes/halifax-now-child/functions.php`

Add this function to properly enqueue Google Fonts. Find the existing
`wp_enqueue_scripts` function or add this as a new one:

```php
// ─── Enqueue Google Fonts: Barlow + Barlow Condensed ───
function halifax_now_enqueue_fonts() {
    wp_enqueue_style(
        'halifax-now-google-fonts',
        'https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Barlow+Condensed:wght@400;600;700;900&family=Barlow:wght@400;500;600&display=swap',
        array(),
        null
    );
}
add_action( 'wp_enqueue_scripts', 'halifax_now_enqueue_fonts' );
```

---

## IMPORTANT NOTES for Cursor:
- Only ADD to the bottom of style.css — do not remove existing CSS
- In functions.php, check if a `halifax_now_enqueue_fonts` function already
  exists before adding — if it does, skip FIX 4
- After making changes, ask Kate to hard refresh (Cmd+Shift+R) to test

**Footer styling so the UI looks consistent**

1. **Inner blocks (groups/columns)**

   Blocks with `.has-background` or classes like `has-white-background-color` inside the footer are overridden to transparent so the default “white block” look doesn’t show on the dark footer.

Actually that could make inner elements transparent and we need the outer footer to stay black. So:
- footer, .site-footer, #colophon, [role="contentinfo"] - keep background black
- footer *, .site-footer *, etc - force background transparent. But then we need to be careful with color - we want text to be light. So:

  footer a { color: rgba(255,255,255,0.65) !important; }

The user wants to know where and how to add the CSS classes `hn-hero` and `hn-cats` in WordPress so that the full-width CSS we wrote will apply to the hero section and the category bar. This is done in the WordPress block editor (Gutenberg) by adding "Additional CSS class(es)" to the relevant blocks.

I should give clear, step-by-step instructions for:
1. Where to do it (WordPress admin → the page or template that contains the homepage content)
2. How to add the class to the hero block (hn-hero)
3. How to add the class to the category bar block (hn-cats)

In block themes like Twenty Twenty-Five, the homepage might be built from:
- A static page (e.g. "Home") that you edit via Pages → Home
- Or a template (e.g. "Home" template or "Index" template) in Appearance → Editor → Templates

_**Agent (default)**_


You add `hn-hero` and `hn-cats` in the **block editor** by giving those classes to the **Group** (or wrapper) blocks that contain the hero and the category bar.

---

### 1. Open the page/template that has the homepage layout

- If your homepage is a **normal Page** (e.g. “Home”):  
  **Pages → Home** (or whatever the homepage is called) → **Edit**.
- If it’s built from a **template**:  
  **Appearance → Editor** → open the template used for the homepage (e.g. “Home” or “Index”).

---

### 2. Add `hn-hero` to the blue hero block

1. Click the **blue hero section** (the one with “FIND YOUR NEXT THING.” and the search bar).
2. In the **right sidebar**, make sure the correct block is selected (often a **Group** block).
3. In the right sidebar, open the **“Advanced”** or **“Block”** section (sometimes under the three-dots menu).
4. Find **“Additional CSS class(es)”** or **“HTML Anchor” / “Advanced”**.
5. In the **Additional CSS class(es)** field, type:  
   `hn-hero`  
   (If there’s already a class, add a space and then `hn-hero`.)
6. Save/Update the page or template.

---

### 3. Add `hn-cats` to the cream category bar block

1. Click the **cream bar** with the category links (LIVE MUSIC, ARTS & CULTURE, etc.).
2. Again, select the **wrapper block** (often a **Group** or **Row**).
3. In the right sidebar, open **Advanced** and find **Additional CSS class(es)**.
4. In that field, type:  
   `hn-cats`  
   (Again, add a space before it if there are existing classes.)
5. Save/Update.

---

### Summary

| What you’re styling | Class to add | Where |
|--------------------|--------------|--------|
| Blue hero (“FIND YOUR NEXT THING.” + search) | `hn-hero` | That block’s **Advanced → Additional CSS class(es)** |
| Cream category chip bar (LIVE MUSIC, etc.)   | `hn-cats`   | That block’s **Advanced → Additional CSS class(es)** |

After saving, do a hard refresh (Cmd+Shift+R) on the homepage. The child theme CSS that uses `.hn-hero` and `.hn-cats` will then apply and pull those sections full width.
